frame_analyses.description: Setup view — Penrose drain on red foam suture block. Standard configuration.

At 10 seconds, both instruments in field near drain with suture and needle visible. Quick entry. A third cylindrical object (possibly instrument handle or scissors resting outside the field) visible in lower frame.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Fast instrument entry consistent with improved sessions. Needle engaged promptly.

frame_analyses.description: At 39 seconds, instruments working near drain. Needle being driven through tissue. Suture thread visible extending from drain downward.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Active needle driving. Instrument coordination appears good.

frame_analyses.description: At 69 seconds, extensive suture loops visible near drain. Both instruments engaged. Multiple wrapping loops suggest the first throw (surgeon's knot) is being formed or tightened.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Throw wrapping visible. The suture geometry suggests a double-wrap pattern consistent with surgeon's knot. Reaching the first throw by ~65s is comparable to post-practice timing.

frame_analyses.description: At 98 seconds, both instruments at drain surface. Suture visible between instruments. Wrapping or tightening in progress for second throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Second throw phase. Instruments appear coordinated. Camera angle makes hand determination difficult.

frame_analyses.description: At 118 seconds, instruments working near drain with suture. Third throw wrapping or tightening.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third throw in progress. The throw phases are individually faster than earlier sessions (~25s each).

frame_analyses.description: At 167 seconds, both instruments engaged near knot. Left instrument holding suture, right instrument appears to be scissors. Suture cutting in progress. Extended cutting phase.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase is consuming excessive time. At 167 seconds, the task should be nearly complete but cutting is still ongoing. This is the primary source of time loss in this attempt.

frame_analyses.description: At 187 seconds, STILL cutting. Left instrument (serrated jaws visible) holding suture tails. Right instrument (scissors) working to cut the second end. Suture tails visible extending upward.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Extremely extended cutting phase — 40+ seconds. This is by far the longest cutting time in the series. The trainee may be having difficulty grasping the suture ends with scissors or achieving proper cutting angle.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 197 seconds. Instruments withdrawn. Completed knot visible on Penrose drain with dark suture thread. The drain slit shows GOOD CLOSURE — edges well-approximated by the knot. Short suture tails visible near the knot site. Drain remains securely on suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Despite being the slowest post-training attempt, the final knot quality appears good. Compact knot, well-approximated slit edges, no visible gap, and appropriately short suture tails. The quality penalty is minimal — the time cost is the primary issue.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 8 shows multiple suture loops consistent with surgeon's knot (double throw). Confidence moderate.
Frame 11 shows throw wrapping. Instrument positions suggest hand exchange from throw 1. Confidence low.

Third throw completed. Final result shows excellent slit closure, confirming adequate knot security. The extended third throw phase (38s) suggests possible difficulty with the wrapping or tightening technique. Confidence moderate based on final result quality.

technique_summary: This fifth video shows a significant time regression — 192 seconds is the slowest post-training attempt and nearly matches the pre-training baseline of 198 seconds. However, the final result quality is actually good: complete slit closure with a compact knot, no visible gap, and short suture tails. The time penalty comes primarily from two sources: (1) an extremely extended cutting phase of ~44 seconds (compared to 18s in post-2), and (2) a prolonged third throw of ~38 seconds. The needle loading (13s) and suture placement (42s) remain comparable to other post-training attempts. This suggests the trainee's knot quality and placement are consistent, but efficiency in suture cutting needs focused practice. The trade-off of slower time for better quality could also indicate the trainee is being more deliberate with knot security.
improvement_suggestions: CRITICAL: Suture cutting took ~44 seconds — this is the largest single phase and the biggest opportunity for improvement. Practice efficient scissors introduction and cutting technique. Target 10-15 seconds.
Third throw took 38 seconds — significantly longer than the 22-28s in previous attempts. Practice smooth wrapping technique for the third throw specifically.
Consider whether the slower pace is deliberate (quality-focused) or indicates difficulty — if deliberate, maintain quality but work on efficiency within each step
The good final result suggests the core technique is solid — the focus should be purely on speed in the cutting and late throw phases
strengths: Best final result quality of any attempt with a visible gap — complete slit closure, compact knot
Needle loading maintained at efficient 13 seconds
Suture placement at 42 seconds is consistent with recent attempts
Drain not avulsed — consistent tissue handling
The quality-vs-speed trade-off suggests growing technical confidence

key_observations: Needle loading stable at 13s across all post-training attempts
Quality improving while speed regressing — suggests deliberate technique focus
